posts.title: Accessibility is a Design Skill
posts.excerpt: The myth that accessible design is boring or purely functional needs to end. Accessibility and beautiful design aren't just compatible — they're inseparable, and the best designers know this.
posts.content:
There's a persistent myth in the design world that I'm done tolerating: the idea that accessible design is inherently boring, clinical, or purely functional.

You've heard the version of this argument. "We need it to look modern." "Our brand is premium." "Accessibility is for later." What these statements really reveal is a misunderstanding of what accessibility is — and what good design actually is.

Accessibility isn't a constraint imposed on creativity. It's a measure of how well your design works for real humans. And real humans are wildly diverse. They use screens in bright sunlight with glare. They're navigating your app one-handed while holding coffee. They've been staring at screens for eight hours and their eyes are tired. They have permanent conditions that affect how they see, hear, or interact with technology.

When you design for that full range of human experience, something interesting happens: your design gets better for everyone. High contrast is easier to read in any lighting condition. Clear navigation structures reduce cognitive load for every user. Large touch targets are easier to hit whether you're using a stylus or a thumb.

## The myth and the evidence

The best editorial and brand design today — Bloomberg Businessweek, the Economist, Apple's product pages — is also highly accessible. High contrast. Clear hierarchy. Purposeful use of space. These aren't accessibility accommodations. They're good design principles that happen to align perfectly with what WCAG recommends.

That alignment is not a coincidence. Both good design and accessible design are rooted in the same place: understanding how humans actually perceive and interact with things.

## What this means in practice

When a company tells me they want their product to look "modern," I ask them what modern means to them. Often they're describing something that trends toward low contrast, ambiguous icons, and motion that serves aesthetics over communication. Those choices aren't modern. They're exclusionary — and they'll age badly.

The designs that stand the test of time are the ones built on solid human factors: legibility, clarity, feedback, and respect for the person using them.

Accessibility is a design skill. Not a compliance checkbox, not a legal risk to manage, not an afterthought to hand off to engineering. It's part of the craft — and one of the most interesting, challenging, and rewarding parts at that.

This site is my attempt to prove it. Every design decision here is made with both beauty and access in mind. I'll be writing here about those decisions, about the broader conversations happening in W3C working groups, about what AI is (and isn't) changing for designers, and about how teams can build this thinking into their culture.

Welcome to the practice.
